coredata.dc:title: Silver nanoparticles coated cellulose-based flexible membrane with excellent UV resistance, high infrared reflection and water resistance for personal thermal management
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jiaxing
Chen, Yongfang
Yue, Xuejie
Zhang, Tao
Li, Yuqi
coredata.dc:description: Designing of a green and multifunctionally integrated cellulose-based flexible wearable material with personal thermoregulation, water and ultraviolet (UV) resistance is essential for the development of personal thermal management and smart textiles. Herein, a hydrophobic silver nanoparticles cellulose-based membrane (H-AgNPs/CEPCM) was prepared through simple solution blending, spin-coating process and chemical vapor modification. The prepared membrane exhibited excellent UV resistance due to the synergistic effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as well as UV-absorbing functional groups. The spin-coated AgNPs layer with high infrared reflectivity has great radiant insulation, and temperature was reduced by 3.4 °C compared with H-CEPCM in indoor environment. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of H-AgNPs/CEPCM were significantly improved by the introduction of amide and ether bonds, as well as a large number of hydrogen bonds. This led to a tensile strength of 23.21 MPa and an elongation at break of 16.57 %, while also providing water resistance. Additionally, the H-AgNPs/CEPCM exhibited outstanding thermal stability and hydrophobicity. This work may provide a feasible and promising strategy for the construction of multifunctional integrated cellulose membrane materials for radiant insulation, outdoor textiles and novel UV protection applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose membrane
UV resistance
Radiation insulation
Flexible
Water resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles
Preparation of the hydrophobic silver nanoparticles cellulose-based membrane
Mechanical properties, water resistance and surface wettability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology of Guilin
GuangXi Key Laboratory of New Energy and Building Energy Saving

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFAA035536 ), GuangXi Key Laboratory of New Energy and Building Energy Saving ( 22-J-21-17 ), and Project of Department of Science and Technology of Guilin ( 20210217-5 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process of H-AgNPs/CEPCM.

Physical images of the cellulose layer (a1) and AgNPs layer (b1) of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, SEM and EDS of the cellulose layer (a2–a3), AgNPs layer (b2–b3) and cross-section (c1–c2) of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, (d) the schematic structure of H-AgNPs/CEPCM.
(a) The AT-FTIR spectrum of CM, CPM, CEPCM, cellulose layer and AgNPs layer of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, (b) the XRD spectra of the cellulose layer and AgNPs layer of H-AgNPs/CEPCM.

(a) Survey scans XPS spectra, (b-e) high-resolution C 1s, O 1s Ag 3d, Si 2p spectra of the cellulose layer and AgNPs layer of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, (f) possible crosslinked polymeric structure of MTMS modifier on the membrane surface.

(a) Stress-strain curves, (b) tensile strength and elongation at break of different membranes, (c) flexibility of the matrix membrane CEPCM, the inset shows the excellent transparency, (d) stretching test of the H-AgNPs/CEPCM with a weight of 1 kg, (e) water resistance test of CM and AgNPs/CEPCM, (f) comparison of stress-strain curves for H-AgNPs/CEPCM before and after immersion in water. (g) WCAs of CM, CEPCM and cellulose layer of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, (h) CAs of different liquids on the cellulose layer of H-AgNPs/CEPCM.

UV resistance evaluations. (a) Schematic illustration of UV resistance of the CEPCM, (b) absorbance and (c) transmittance of different membranes in the UV–visible band, (d) demonstration of different membranes used to shield from UV light.

Thermal performance evaluations. (a) Schematic of experimental device for the radiative heating measurement, (b) comparison of the temperature for the bare skin, cotton cloth, H-CEPCM and H-AgNPs/CEPCM, (c) thermal image of cotton cloth, H-CEPCM and H-AgNPs/CEPCM, (d) schematic diagram of radiation thermal insulation.

head.ce:title: Silver nanoparticles coated cellulose-based flexible membrane with excellent UV resistance, high infrared reflection and water resistance for personal thermal management

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal & Materials, Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing of a green and multifunctionally integrated cellulose-based flexible wearable material with personal thermoregulation, water and ultraviolet (UV) resistance is essential for the development of personal thermal management and smart textiles. Herein, a hydrophobic silver nanoparticles cellulose-based membrane (H-AgNPs/CEPCM) was prepared through simple solution blending, spin-coating process and chemical vapor modification. The prepared membrane exhibited excellent UV resistance due to the synergistic effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as well as UV-absorbing functional groups. The spin-coated AgNPs layer with high infrared reflectivity has great radiant insulation, and temperature was reduced by 3.4 °C compared with H-CEPCM in indoor environment. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of H-AgNPs/CEPCM were significantly improved by the introduction of amide and ether bonds, as well as a large number of hydrogen bonds. This led to a tensile strength of 23.21 MPa and an elongation at break of 16.57 %, while also providing water resistance. Additionally, the H-AgNPs/CEPCM exhibited outstanding thermal stability and hydrophobicity. This work may provide a feasible and promising strategy for the construction of multifunctional integrated cellulose membrane materials for radiant insulation, outdoor textiles and novel UV protection applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) light, as part of solar radiation, consists mainly of three different wavelengths of light: UVA (320–400 nm), UVB (280–320 nm) and UVC (220–280 nm) (Wang et al., 2017). Prolonged exposure to UV radiation can cause various skin issues and increase the risk of cancer (Dickerson et al., 1997; Yang, Gao, Zuo, Deng, & Dai, 2019). However, ordinary fabrics are difficult to protect the skin from UV radiation. Therefore, it is crucial to search a simple and feasible strategy to cope with the increase of UV radiation in surroundings. Currently, UV shielding agents can be classified into two types. The first type is organic UV absorbers such as salicylic acid and benzophenone. The second type is made up of inorganic UV reflectors like titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, and talc (Bisht, Kumar, Maity, Lahiri, & Lahiri, 2019; Xie et al., 2015). However, these traditional agents have drawbacks such as high cost, possible decomposition for a long time, excessive nanoparticle loading and agglomeration, and skin irritation (Sadeghifar, Venditti, Jur, Gorga, & Pawlak, 2017). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of fluorescent carbon-based material with a size of <10 nm, have good water solubility, abundant functional groups, low toxicity and biocompatibility (Hao et al., 2019). They have been widely used in cellular imaging, fluorescent probes, catalysts and sensors (Wareing, Gentile, & Phan, 2021). Moreover, CQDs have a distinctive feature of strong absorption peaks in the UV region, resulting from the π-π jump of the CC bond (Hess et al., 2017). Thus, CQDs as a new environmentally friendly UV absorber for the preparation of UV protective materials program is feasible and effective.

In recent years, cellulose-based wearable materials have attracted much attention for their reproducibility, biocompatibility and environmental friendliness (Bai et al., 2023; Ding, Yang, & Tang, 2023; Zhao et al., 2021). Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are nanoscale cellulose derived from natural fibers, possessing nano-structural properties that offer advantages such as high crystallinity, high modulus of elasticity, and unique optical properties compared to traditional cellulose (Gomri, Cretin, & Semsarilar, 2022; Jin et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2021). In addition, the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups on surface facilitates chemical modification and ensures adhesion of the coating material (Ding, Tang, & Zhu, 2022; Huang et al., 2021). However, the rigidity of CNCs and limited mobility between individual crystals lead to brittleness of the material, thus limiting applications in the field of flexible wearable (Jaekel, Kluge, Tröger-Müller, Tutus, & Filonenko, 2022). Although cross-linking based on small molecules improves the strength of membrane, it is difficult to improve its flexibility. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) is a synthetic macromolecule polymer synthesized from aziridine by cationic polymerization (Wang, Li, & Lu, 2015), containing a large number of amino groups. The crosslinked mesh structure is formed through the reaction between PEI and CNCs, which is expected to improve the mechanical properties of composites (Li, Ju, & Zhang, 2020; Song, Liu, Zhu, & Li, 2019). Moreover, the application of cellulose-based wearable materials is also limited due to lack of water resistance (Huang, Chen, Liang, Yang, & Shen, 2020; Mao, Wei, Gong, Wang, & Ding, 2019). New hydrogen bonds are formed between the water molecules and the hydroxyl groups surrounding the cellulose on contacting. This process results in the breaking of the hydrogen bonds within the cellulose, leading to dissolution and decomposition (Yang, Zhang, et al., 2019). Although cellulose-based materials with water resistance have achieved some satisfactory properties (Liu, Cui, Tan, & Wang, 2021; Zhou et al., 2023), it is a crucial issue to realize multifunctionality of cellulose-based wearable materials.

There are three main ways in which the human body exchanges heat with the surrounding environment through textiles: heat conduction and convection, thermal radiation and sweat evaporation (Peng & Cui, 2020). Thermal radiation is crucial for dissipating heat from the body and needs to be accurately controlled for keeping warm (Kou et al., 2021; He et al., 2023). However, ordinary textiles with high infrared emissivity (0.75–0.9) and very low infrared reflectivity (0.25–0.1) do not effectively inhibit body heat dissipation (Yue, He, Zhang, Yang, & Qu, 2020), resulting in the weak radiative insulation. Passive heated textiles (PHT), a key component of personal thermal management systems, capture solar energy while suppressing the dissipation of thermal radiation from the body (Chen, Zhao, et al., 2022; Tang, Lyu, Gao, Jia, & Ma, 2023). This is achieved by enhancing the internal mid-infrared reflectivity or reducing the external mid-infrared emissivity. Metal nanowires and particles have attracted a lot of attention as a good infrared reflector. Among various metal materials, silver is an excellent choice due to high infrared reflection, resistance to acids and alkalis, and oxidation stability (Yue, Zhang, Yang, Qiu, & Li, 2018). Yue et al. prepared multifunctional silver nanoparticles cellulose fiber film to achieve high thermal insolating (Yue, Zhang, Yang, Qiu, Li, et al., 2019). Gu et al. reported a laminated cellulose hybrid membrane with Ni@Ag-NWs coating has a low infrared emissivity (10 %) (Gu, Huang, Qiu, Yang, & Zhang, 2020). Therefore, it is of great significant to explore a simple and effective strategy for enhancing thermal insulation.

Thus, we hypothesized that the introduction of suitable cross-linking agents and functional components may address the brittleness and sensitivity to moisture of nanocellulose materials, while promising to enable the integration of personal thermoregulation and UV resistance into cellulose-based flexible wearable materials. To verify this hypothesis, a hydrophobic silver nanoparticles cellulose-based membrane (H-AgNPs/CEPCM) was successfully prepared through a simple and feasible method without complicated microfabrication. The cellulose-based membrane (CEPCM) was obtained by solution blending. The matrix of the membrane was C-CNCs, while PEI and ECH served as cross-linking agents, and CQDs acted as novel UV absorbers. Then, effective thermal insulation was achieved via spin-coating a layer of AgNPs on the surface of the membrane, followed by a simple chemical vapor modification to obtain a hydrophobic surface. Furthermore, the UV resistance of the prepared membrane was significantly increased by adding CQDs and PEI, thereby protecting skin from UV radiation. The significantly enhanced mechanical properties and water resistance of the H-AgNPs/CEPCM enable it suitable for more complex applications. This research offers a feasible and promising strategy for developing multifunctional integrated cellulose-based membrane materials for applications such as radiant insulation, outdoor textiles, and innovative UV protection.

Carboxylated cellulose nanocrystals dispersions (C-CNCs) were offered by Guilin Qihong Technology Co (The fiber diameter is in the range of 4–10 nm and the length is 100–500 nm, content: 2.8 wt%, density: 1.06 g/cm3). Silver nitrate (AgNO3, ≥99.8 %), citric acid (CA), ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2, ≥99 %), acetone (CH3COCH3, ≥99 %), ethanol (CH3COOH, ≥99.7 %), and Rhodamine B (RB) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw = 58,000, K29-32), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, 99.9 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw = 1800), epichlorohydrin (ECH) and methyltrimethoxysilane (MTMS) were supplied by Aladdin Reagents Ltd. Deionized water was obtained from the laboratory. All the reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxy-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized through bottom-up pyrolysis, using CA as precursor and carbonization by air-insulated heating. 10 g of CA powder and 5 mL of water were in a hydrothermal reactor, then put into a 200 °C drying oven and heated for 3 h. The products were washed by centrifugation with ethanol. The preparation process is shown in Fig. S1. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were synthesized via the polyol reduction method, employing PVP as capping agent. Firstly, 0.68 g of AgNO3 was added to 25 mL of EG and sonicated for 30 min to form solution A. Subsequently, 0.71 g of PVP and 7 mg of CuCl2·2H2O were added to 25 mL of EG and stirred well to form solution B at 25 °C. Solutions A and B were mixed well and transferred to a reaction kettle, where they were heated to 160 °C for 2.5 h. The resulting products were washed three times by centrifugation (8000 r/min) with acetone and ethanol to obtain the AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the hydrophobic silver nanoparticles cellulose-based membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the preparation of the hydrophobic silver nanoparticles cellulose-based membrane (H-AgNPs/CEPCM) undergoes two main steps. Initially, 20 g of C-CNCs, 0.2 g of PEI, 0.05 g of CQDs and 2 mL of ECH were mixed well at 30 °C in a flask. The mixture was then poured onto a glass petri dish and directly dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain the cellulose-based membrane (CEPCM). Subsequently, the AgNPs solution was spin-coated onto the membrane and dried, yielding the AgNPs/CEPCM. The hydrophobic modification of the membrane was achieved through a simple chemical vapor process. In this process, the AgNPs/CEPCM was placed in an airtight beaker with two 2 mL bottles of MTMS and water. The beaker was heated at 80 °C for 6 h to induce volatilization of MTMS, which then grafted onto the membrane. Unreacted MTMS was subsequently removed by placing the membrane in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The thickness of the membranes was approximately 0.21–0.24 mm. In addition, the other compared membranes were also prepared according to the same process in Table S1.

The morphology and structure of both sides of H-AgNPs/CEPCM were analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Japan). The dimension of C-CNCs was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F). The elemental composition was analyzed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) measurements. The crystal structure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument (Shimadzu XRD-6100). The chemical structure and composition of both sides of H-AgNPs/CEPCM were determined by total reflection/Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Thermo Nicolet 6700) in the scan range of 400–4000 cm−1. The molecular structure and atomic valence states of the material are analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi). The mechanical properties of the different samples with a dumbbell shape were tested on a universal tensile testing machine (UTM4503BLXY) with a tensile speed of 2 mm/min. The average values of the mechanical properties (tensile strength and elongation at break) were obtained by measuring the same sample at least three times. The contact angles (CAs) were measured using a contact angle tester (OCA50). Droplets (5 μL) were dropped at three different locations on the sample surface to obtain the average value of the CAs. The thermal stability of different samples was investigated by TGA (STA 449 F5 Jupiter) from 25 °C to 700 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

Absorbance and transmittance of solutions and membranes were measured using a visible-ultraviolet spectrophotometer (LAMBSDA 750). In order to visualize the UV shielding performance, the different membranes with the same size (2 × 2 cm2) and thickness (0.22 ± 0.01 mm) were covered on top of a transparent glass bottle containing water solution of RB (consistent concentration of solution in each bottle). The RB normally shows bright red color under UV light. Thus, the color shades of the different bottles indicate the amount of UV light gained to evaluate the UV resistance.

Informed consent was provided to the participating subjects prior to testing. The radiative insulation of H-AgNPs/CEPCM is achieved by reflecting infrared radiation through the AgNPs coating on the inside. In order to investigate the passive heating performance, cotton cloth, the H-CEPCM and H-AgNPs/CEPCM were cut into 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 shapes and placed on the back of the hand, where the AgNPs surface was facing the back of the hand. The whole test was performed at 28 °C, while the surface temperature was recorded with infrared camera. In addition, a simple indoor thermal measurement device was designed to further investigate the radiant heating performance.

The infrared radiation property was significantly influenced by the microstructure and morphology of the material. Therefore, SEM was used to investigated the morphologies of both sides of H-AgNPs/CEPCM. Fig. 2 (a1) shows that the cellulose layer is darker due to the effect of AgNPs coating. In Fig. 2(a2), the surface of cellulose layer appears smooth and flat, without fibrous structure. This smooth surface is beneficial for the formation of a well-structured cellulose membrane. Moreover, the structure and morphology of the C-CNCs were further characterized through TEM. As shown in Fig. S2, the length is 100–200 nm and the inset shows the C-CNCs water dispersion. From Fig. 2(b1), the other side of H-AgNPs/CEPCM appears an off-white surface due to the presence of AgNPs layer. In Fig. 2(b2), the surface exhibits a dense and regular arrangement of AgNPs, forming a coating that reflects infrared radiation. Silver typically exhibits extremely low infrared emissivity and high infrared reflectivity, indicating that the H-AgNPs/CEPCM has superior radiant insulation. Fig. S3 shows that a narrow diameter distribution of the prepared AgNPs, ranging from 80 to 250 nm, with an average diameter of 150 nm. The inset is a physical picture of an ethanol solution containing AgNPs. EDS analysis was conducted to determine the chemical composition of both sides of H-AgNPs/CEPCM. Fig. S4 shows the original SEM image of the cellulose layer, AgNPs layer and cross-section for EDS. The results in Fig. 2(a3) show the presence of C, N, O and Si elements on the skeleton surface of the cellulose membrane. The Si element is only derived from MTMS, indicating the MTMS was successfully grafted to obtain a hydrophobic surface. Additionally, the distribution of Ag element of AgNPs layer is quite dense in Fig. 2(b3), indicating a high Ag content. Fig. 2(d) shows the schematic structure of H-AgNPs/CEPCM. To visually illustrate the layered structure, a cross-section analysis was performed. Fig. 2(c1) clearly exhibits the distribution area of AgNPs layer and cellulose layer, with close contact between layers. Furthermore, EDS mapping of the cross-section in Fig. 2(c2) confirms that Ag element is uniformly distributed on one side of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, while C, O, Si elements are distributed on the other side.

The ATR-FTIR was used to analyze the surface functional groups and chemical structure of the different samples. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the broad band at 3100–3700 cm−1 corresponds to the superposition of OH and NH stretching vibrations in C-CNCs and PEI. The spectrum of CPM showed new characteristic absorption peaks at 1605.1 cm−1 (CO, NH), 1478.6 cm−1 (CN stretching vibration, amide III absorption band), 1426.8 cm−1 (−NH2 variable angle vibration, amide II absorption band) (He et al., 2020). Moreover, the peaks at 2920 and 2838 cm−1 were attributed to enhanced CH stretching (antisymmetric, symmetric) vibrations in the large amount of CH2. These emerging characteristic peaks indicated PEI was successfully grafted onto C-CNCs. In addition, the absence of the typical peaks of epoxy groups at 1250 or 910 cm−1 also suggests that C-CNCs and PEI reacted with ECH (Zhang et al., 2017). The typical characteristic peaks of CPM and CEPCM are shifted to lower wavelengths, verifying the presence of a large number of hydrogen bonds in the structure. Meanwhile, the decrease in the intensity of the peak at around 1420 cm−1 for CPM and CEPCM suggested a decrease in crystallinity (Chen, Song, et al., 2022). The absorption peaks of the cellulose layer of H- AgNPs/CEPCM were all weakened due to the effect of AgNPs coating. But the significant absorption peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 1020 cm−1 are attributed to Si-O-C and SiO stretching vibrations indicating the MTMS successfully modifies membrane to achieve hydrophobic effect (Li et al., 2023). Unlike other spectra, no obvious characteristic peaks appeared in the AgNPs layer, and even the original absorption peaks of cellulose disappeared, indicating a good infrared shielding effect.

The composition and crystal structure of both sides of H-AgNPs/CEPCM were investigated by XRD in Fig. 3(b). In the spectra of the cellulose layer, four significant characteristic peaks are shown at 2θ = 14.76°, 16.28°, 22.65°, and 34.84°, which correspond to the (1
1 ¯
0), (110), (200), and (004) planes of cellulose Iβ (Yue et al., 2019). And the AgNPs layer shows four typical characteristic peaks, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of silver and matching the face-centered cubic silver crystals (JCPDS 04-0783) (Zhao, Chen, Yue, Zhang, & Li, 2023). In addition, characteristic peaks of other purities (e.g., Ag2O) were not observed in the spectra, indicating that the silver is very pure. The dense AgNPs coating facilitates increased infrared reflectivity and electrical conductivity of membrane. The XRD spectra results indicated the multifunctional integrated H-AgNPs/CEPCM have been successfully prepared.

The surface composition and chemical state of both sides of the H-AgNPs/CEPCM were analyzed via XPS. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the cellulose layer shows XPS C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Si 2s, Si 2s, Si 2p elemental peaks and no Ag-related peaks, indicating the absence of AgNPs infiltration. But the AgNPs layer has three more major peaks as Ag 3s, Ag 3p, and Ag 3d. The peak intensities of Ag 3d and Ag 3p are higher than those of C 1s and O 1s, indicating that the higher loading of AgNPs. Furthermore, the N 1s peak at 400 eV indicates the presence of NH in the formed amide bond. To further understand the reactions and bonding during the preparation process, high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O 1s, Ag 3d and Si 2p were analyzed. In Fig. 4(b), the typical high-resolution C 1s spectra of cellulose layer can be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to C−C, C−O, and CO with binding energies of 284.8, 285.8 and 286.6 eV, respectively. The electronegativity of the atoms changed due to the AgNPs deposited on the membrane, and the Full Width Half Maximum (FWHM) the CC of AgNPs layer became smaller and narrower. Meanwhile, the binding energy of O1 s changed from 532.4 eV to 532.1 eV, and the FWHM of the O1s peak was also narrower than that of the peak on the cellulose layer in Fig. 4(c). This is mainly due to the fact that the addition of AgNPs reduces the electron density, leading to a lower binding energy (Han et al., 2012). In Fig. 4(d), the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 core level binding energies on the AgNPs layer appear at 367.8 and 373.9 eV, and these values are in good agreement with the standard values corresponding to bulk silver metal. The relatively low half-peak full width (1.2 eV) of the Ag 3d5/2 peak suggests the absence of other Ag valence states, which is consistent with the XRD mapping response. In Fig. 4(e), the high-resolution Si 2p spectra shows two peaks for SiC (102.3 eV) and SiO (102.9 eV) (Chen et al., 2024), which further indicates that the silane coupling agent MTMS was successfully grafted. Fig. 4(f) shows that a crosslinked polymeric structure may be formed on the surface of the cellulose-based membrane by hydrolytic polymerization of MTMS in the presence of water. In Fig. S5, the increased G (85.23 %) and decreased Q (127.52 %) value indirectly proved the increased cross-linking degree, which further confirmed the successful grafting of MTMS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, water resistance and surface wettability
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the practical applications of functional wearable materials, mechanical properties and flexibility are particularly important. The detailed mechanical properties of different membranes were further evaluated by tensile testing. In Fig. 5 (a–b), the tensile strength of pure CM is 9.81 MPa and the elongation at break is 5.41 %, which leads to a brittle membrane material with very poor flexibility. After adding PEI, the elongation at break of the CPM was significantly improved to 22.16 %, showing excellent toughness, which was mainly due to the reaction between the amino group in PEI and the carboxyl group in C-CNCs to form amide bonds, as well as cellulose layer formed abundant hydrogen bonds (Lamm et al., 2021). The elongation at break of the CEPCM decreased by 6.93 %, and the tensile strength reached 19.51 MPa. This was mainly attributed to the cross-linking of C-CNCs and PEI by ECH through the -OH binding site, which further increased the cross-linking density. This degree of cross-linking facilitates the formation of a denser structure, which enables more effective resistance to external forces (Bangar et al., 2022). The tensile strength of the H-AgNPs/CEPCM reached a maximum of 23.21 MPa, while the elongation at break was maintained at 16.57 %, which was increased by 137 % and 206 % compared with the pure CM. This is mainly contributed to the successful grafting of MTMS, resulting in further improvement of the mechanical properties, while the AgNPs layer provides some enhancement. In addition, the TGA and DTG curves in Fig. S6 showed the excellent thermal stability of H-AgNPs/CEPCM, which also confirms the presence of cross-linking structures.

The comfort of wearer is also affected by the flexibility of the membrane material. As shown as in Fig. 5(c), the matrix membrane CEPCM was folded into a windmill shape, indicating that the membrane has superior flexibility. The inset also showed its excellent transparency. Moreover, the H-AgNPs/CEPCM exhibited high tensile strength to hold up to 1 kg steadily in Fig. 5(d). The results showed the excellent tensile strength and flexibility, which is crucial to improve the wearer comfort and extend the service life of the membrane material. In addition, the water resistance was evaluated by immersing them in water, and the results were shown in Fig. 5(e). After 2 days of static immersion, the pure CM showed some dissolution, while the AgNPs/CEPCM remained intact after the static immersion test, and the liquid in the bottle was still transparent, with no collapse or detachment detected. This is mainly due to the formation of a crosslinked structure capable of resisting the erosion of solvent. In Fig. 5(f), the tensile strength and elongation at break of the membrane after immersion were 21.91 MPa and 16.06 %, which could achieve 94 % and 97 % of the original membrane. Thus, the H-AgNPs/CEPCM with strong water resistance can be adapted to more complex application scenarios.

Personal thermal comfort is also affected by the wettability of the membrane. The wettability of different membranes was investigated via a water contact angle measurement. As shown in Fig. 5(g), the pure CM has a water contact angle (WCA) of 52.8°, while the WCA of CEPCM dropped to only 28.6°, which is attributed to the large number of hydrophilic groups in PEI and CQDs. The cellulose layer of H-AgNPs/CEPCM showed excellent hydrophobicity, with a WCA of 128.2°. In addition to pure water, Fig. 5(h) showed the CAs of tea, milk, coffee and fruit juice were all over 120°, which also meets the different scenarios of application. Additionally, a comparison analysis of the main properties or functions in this work with other similar materials was demonstrated in Table S2.

Wearable fabrics with excellent UV resistance are essential for personal health. Fig. 6(a) shows the schematic diagram of the prepared membrane for UV resistance. As shown in Fig. 6(b–c), the absorbance and transmittance of the different membranes were tested using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The pure CM and CEM was virtually non-absorbent of UV light, but the UV absorbance of the CPM and CCM was improved to different degrees. The CEPCM showed the highest UV absorbance in the UV band, which was mainly attributed to the strong UV absorption ability of CQDs, with the best UV absorption peak at 360 nm. In Fig. S7, the excitation emission spectra and excitation-dependent PL spectra of the CQDs also confirm such a scenario. Moreover, the UV-absorbing capacity was further improved due to a large number of UV-absorbing groups. In Fig. 6(c), the CEPCM has an extremely low UV transmittance and a great visible light transmittance, indicating that a large amount of UV light is absorbed by the CEPCM and does not come into contact with the skin. In order to further investigate the UV absorption principle, the absorbance of CQDs solution (0.1 wt%–0.5 wt%) and PEI water solution (1 wt%–5 wt%) was tested in Fig. S8. The absorbance of both solutions increased with the increase of the concentration, which indicates better UV-resistant effect. Thus, the simultaneous addition of both significantly improves the UV shielding property of the membrane. The results in Fig. 6(d) further demonstrated superior UV resistance. The color of bottles covering different membrane s is consistent without UV irradiation. In contrast, the clear color contrast can be seen under UV irradiation. When not covered with any membrane or covered with pure CM, the bottle showed strong red, indicating a large amount of UV transmission. The fluorescence effect of bottles covered with CPM and CCM was reduced to varying degrees. Besides, the bottle covered with CEPCM displayed the weakest fluorescence. This is due to the fact that the synergistic effect of both PEI and CQDs maximizes UV absorption, leading to the least amount of UV acquired by the RB solution. Thus, the prepared membrane with excellent UV shielding performance has great potential in UV protective materials and outdoor textiles.

Increased infrared emissivity is effective for the passive heating effect of textiles (Gu, Fan, Xu, Shou, & Zhao, 2023). The AgNPs with high infrared reflectivity are ideal for enhancing the thermal insulation capability of cellulose-based membrane. To further investigate the radiant heating performance, a simple indoor thermal measurement device is designed in Fig. 7(a), which mainly consisted of an electric heater, a thermocouple, simulated skin and samples. The black insulating tape is ideal for simulating skin because its infrared emissivity is similar to that of skin. The temperature of the electric heater was maintained at 37 °C by adjusting the DC voltage, and the real-time temperature of the simulated skin was recorded by the thermocouple (under the test condition: the stable environmental temperature of 26.5 °C). In Fig. 7(b), the H-AgNPs/CEPCM covering the simulated skin (the AgNPs layer facing inward) displayed a temperature of 37.2 °C, which was much higher than that of the bare skin (31.2 °C), cotton cloth-covered (33.6 °C) and H-CEPCM-covered (32.3 °C).

In order to further reveal the actual radiant insulation performance, the cotton cloth, H-CEPCM and H-AgNPs/CEPCM were covered on the back of the hand. After the temperature was stabilized, the thermal image in Fig. 7(c) showed the different interception of thermal radiation for the three samples. The H-AgNPs/CEPCM showed a lowest temperature of 29.3 °C, which was 2.8 °C and 3.4 °C lower than that of cotton cloth and H-CEPCM respectively. The results demonstrated the more excellent thermal insulation properties. This is largely attributed to the fact that silver has a very high infrared reflectivity, which inhibits the dissipation of thermal radiation from the body, resulting in only a small amount of infrared radiation being transmitted to the outdoor environment. Accordingly, the microenvironmental temperature between the skin and membrane is greatly increased. The schematic diagram of the radiant insulation performance without energy input is shown Fig. 7(d). Due to the weak infrared reflective properties of H-CEPCM, most of the infrared radiation emitted by the human body can easily pass through the membrane, resulting in the loss of body heat. In contrast, the AgNPs layer with high infrared reflectivity can be used as a reflective layer to reflect most of the infrared radiation back to the body, thus achieving precise localized warmth. The results showed that the H-AgNPs/CEPCM exhibits excellent radiative insulation performance, which provides a strong guarantee for thermal comfort in cold environments.

In summary, a multifunctional H-AgNPs/CEPCM with outstanding UV resistance and radiant insulation was prepared through a simple and feasible strategy of combining solution blending, spin-coating process and chemical steam modification. The prepared membrane showed excellent UV resistance due to functional groups such as amide and amino groups, as well as CQDs that absorb a large amount of UV light. The AgNPs layer with high infrared radiation reflection effectively reflected personal heat radiation, and the temperature was reduced by 3.4 °C compared with H-CEPCM in indoor environment. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of H-AgNPs/CEPCM were significantly enhanced through cross-linking reactions of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in C-CNCs with amino groups in PEI and epoxy groups in ECH. This cross-linking structure overcame the issues of poor flexibility and water intolerance commonly found in nanocellulose materials. The tensile strength of H-AgNPs/CEPCM was 23.21 MPa, while the elongation at break was 16.57 %, which were 137 % and 206 % higher than those of the pure C-CNCs membrane, respectively. In addition, the H-AgNPs/CEPCM exhibited excellent thermal stability and hydrophobicity. Thus, the proposed multi-functional nanocellulose membrane derived from green biomass holds great potential applications such as personal thermal management, UV protective clothing and novel UV resistant products.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFAA035536), GuangXi Key Laboratory of New Energy and Building Energy Saving (22-J-21-17), and Project of Department of Science and Technology of Guilin (20210217-5).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2024.121778.
